introduction: African trypanosomiasis is an acute or chronic disease caused by Trypanosoma brucei namely T. brucei rhodesiense (East Africa) or T. brucei gambiense (West Africa). The disease is transmitted to humans by the bites of infected tsetse fly (Glossina genus).

features: A nodule or chancre following a bite
Significant posterior cervical (Winterbottom sign) and supraclavicular lymphadenopathy
Occurs six months to several years later
Characterized by behavioural changes with hallucinations, delusions, and disturbances of sleep with drowsiness during the day and terminating with stupor

investigations: Peripheral blood film for the detection of trypanosomes
Rapid Card Agglutination Trypanosomiasis Test (CATT) for antibody detection

drug: Early stage: Suramin (treatment of T.b. rhodesiense). Adult and child: 5 mg/kg on day 1, 10 mg/kg on day 3, and 20 mg/kg on days 5, 11, 17, 23 and 30
Early stage: Pentamidine (treatment of T. b. gambiense). Dose: 4 mg/kg/day IM or IV (diluted in saline in 2-hour infusions) x 7 days

Late stage: Melarsoprol (treatment of both gambiense and rhodesiense infections). Adult: 2.0 - 3.6 mg/kg intravenously in 3 divided doses for 3 days, followed 1 week later with 3.6 mg/kg intravenously in 3 divided doses for 3 days 10 - 21 days later: 3.6 mg/kg intravenously in 3 divided doses for 3 days.

Late stage: Nifurtimox-eflornithine combination therapy (NECT) - (treatment of T. b. gambiense). Nifurtimox 15 mg/kg per day orally in three doses x 10 days, and eflornithine 400 mg/kg/day IV in two 2-hour infusions (each dose diluted in 250 mL of water for injection) x 7 days. Eflornithine in children weighing <10 kg: dilute in 50 mL of water for injection. Children weighing 10 - 25 kg: dilute in 100 mL of water for injection.
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Urine should be examined for casts and protein before and after treatment with suramin.
Lumbar puncture follow-up for at least 1 year after treatment with melasoprol is required.
There is no test of cure for African trypanosomiasis.
After treatment, patients should be closely followed for 24 months and monitored for relapse.
Recurrence of symptoms will require examination of body fluids, including CSF, to detect the presence of trypanosomes.

prevention: Vector control by selective clearing of vegetation and use of insecticides.
